summary: Patrick announces he's identified a new software product opportunity while working on Bingo Card Creator. The problem he encountered meets his criteria for a viable micro-ISV product: it requires technical skill, affects many non-technical users, and has a solve-once-apply-everywhere model. He commits to a 30-day development sprint starting June 1st, inspired by the Business of Software forums' 30 Day Challenge, with a goal of offering the product to paying customers by July 1st. He invites other developers to join him in an informal parallel development effort, blogging about progress together.
key_points: A good micro-ISV product opportunity has specific characteristics: requires programming effort, is hard for non-technical users, affects many people, blocks revenue, and has a solve-once-apply-everywhere model
Artificial deadlines and peer accountability are effective motivators for side-project development
After shipping one product, the fear of being cloned diminishes significantly — execution matters more than secrecy
The barrier to entry for community building should be as low as possible — just blog about it rather than building infrastructure
money_quotes: It requires programming effort, it is really difficult for a technically disinclined person, it affects a lot of link-chucking people, it gets in the way of some people making money, it has a solve-once apply-anywhere model to it.
So, basically, it screams 'I Am A Viable uISV Problem'.
I started to see code and database schematics and promotional schemes. (Hey, THAT certainly didn't happen last time… guess I'm learning…)
I've got little interest for anything which purports to be for complete Internet-commerce newbies, as it smacks of Make Money Online and I have my sights a little higher than $10.
We'll have plenty of issues building websites without having to build a website about building our website.
Having done this once I'm much less worried about getting cloned these days than I used to be.
key_insight: A viable micro-ISV product opportunity reveals itself when you encounter a problem that is technically solvable, painful for non-technical users, widely applicable, and blocks people from making money.
